lead: An accurate quote is the foundation of a stress-free move, and yet so many people accept a vague figure over the phone and then feel blindsided on the day. After twenty years of quoting moves across Suffolk, we know exactly what separates a reliable quote from a hopeful guess. The good news is that getting an accurate one is largely in your hands, and it comes down to giving the firm a true picture of your move. Here is how to make sure the number you are quoted is the number you can trust.

sections.body:
The single most important step is to have your home properly surveyed, either in person or by a video walkthrough, before you accept a quote. A survey lets the firm see the real volume of your belongings and the genuine access at both ends, which is simply impossible to capture in a phone call. In our experience, a quote without a survey is a quote built on hope, and hope has a way of turning into extra charges on moving day.

A good survey is not intrusive or lengthy. We walk through each room, note the furniture and the rough number of boxes, and look in the places people forget: the loft, the garage, the shed and the cupboards that quietly hold half a household. We also look at how we will get everything out, where the lorry can park, and whether any stairs or doorways will slow things down. From that, an accurate figure can be built with confidence.

Video surveys have made this easier than ever, especially for busy households or longer-distance moves. A guided walk around on your phone lets us see everything we need without anyone having to find a mutually convenient morning. Whichever way it is done, the principle is the same: let the firm see your move with their own eyes, because that is the only thing that turns a guess into a quote you can rely on.

sections.h: Show Them Everything, Including the Loft
sections.body:
The biggest cause of inaccurate quotes is not dishonesty by the firm; it is belongings the firm never saw. It is astonishing how much lives in a loft, a garage or a garden shed, and how easily it is overlooked because we have stopped noticing it. When that hidden volume only appears on moving day, the carefully built quote no longer matches the job, and that is when problems start. The fix is simple: show everything.

So when the surveyor visits, open the loft hatch, unlock the shed, and point out the items you might secretly be hoping to forget about. Mention the bikes, the garden machinery, the boxes of Christmas decorations, the freezer in the utility room and the contents of the wardrobe you never open. None of this is judged; it is simply counted, so the crew and the van that turn up are the right size for the job you actually have.

The same applies to the big, awkward items that shape a move more than their number suggests. A piano, a full-size American fridge-freezer, a heavy wardrobe that will not come apart, or a glass-topped table all need extra thought, and flagging them at the survey means we plan for them properly rather than meeting them as a surprise. An accurate quote is, above all, a complete one, and completeness depends on you holding nothing back.

sections.h: Be Honest About Access at Both Ends
sections.body:
Access is the factor people most often gloss over, and it can have a real effect on time and therefore cost. Carrying everything up three flights is slower than a straight run into a bungalow, and a lorry that cannot park near the door means every box is walked further. The trap is that people describe the easy end and forget the difficult one, but a move is only as smooth as its harder half, so both must be assessed honestly.

Tell the firm about anything unusual at either property. A long gravel drive, a low cottage doorway, a tight period staircase, a shared driveway, a gated development, a height-restricted car park, or a lift that may or may not fit a sofa all matter. These are not problems when they are known in advance; they simply let us bring the right vehicle, crew and equipment. They become problems only when they are discovered on the morning, with the clock running.

Be especially careful about the new home, which the firm may not have visited at all. If you have details about parking, stairs or access there, share them, because an accurate quote depends on understanding both ends of the journey. A move quoted on a perfect old home and an unknown new one is only half assessed, and the unseen half is exactly where unexpected time, and unexpected cost, tends to hide.

sections.h: Decide Your Services Before You Quote
sections.body:
An accurate quote also depends on knowing what you actually want done. Packing is the biggest choice: will you pack everything yourself, hand the lot to our crew, or split the difference by doing the easy rooms and leaving the fragile kitchen and awkward items to us? Each option changes the figure, so deciding before you quote means the price reflects the real scope of work rather than a vague assumption that has to be revised later.

Think too about the extras that genuinely save hassle, and decide which you want included. Dismantling and reassembling beds and wardrobes, handling a piano or a large appliance, disconnecting and reconnecting white goods, or supplying boxes and materials are all sensible additions for many moves. Naming them up front keeps the quote complete, whereas adding them as afterthoughts on the day muddies a price that was otherwise clear and agreed.

sections.h: Get It in Writing and Ask What's Included
sections.body:
Once you have a figure, get it in writing, and never settle for a number agreed only over the phone. A written quote protects everyone, because it removes any room for misunderstanding about what was agreed before the work began. A reputable firm provides this as a matter of course and is glad to talk you through it line by line, so you understand not just the total but what sits behind it.

Then ask the simple, important questions. Are materials included? Is dismantling and reassembly part of the price? Is the protection of your belongings covered, and does that protection extend to any storage? Is there any charge for waiting time, stairs or a long carry, and if so, on what basis? Every move we carry out is fully covered, and we set all of this out in writing precisely so you know exactly what you are paying for.

Finally, use these answers to compare quotes properly. A higher figure that includes materials, dismantling and proper protection may well be better value than a lower one that quietly omits them, so make sure you are comparing like with like rather than headline numbers. When you understand what each price actually covers, you can choose with real confidence, which is the whole purpose of getting an accurate quote in the first place.

sections.h: Your Part in an Accurate Quote
sections.body:
The pattern running through all of this is straightforward: the accuracy of your quote depends largely on the honesty and completeness of the picture you give. Show everything, describe both ends truthfully, decide your services, and get the answer in writing. Do that, and the figure you are quoted will be the figure you pay, with no unwelcome surprises to add to the strain of moving day.

We are always glad to make this easy. Our quotes are free and carry no obligation, we will survey your home in person or by video to whatever suits you, and we will set everything out clearly in writing, including the fact that your move is fully covered. If you would like an accurate quote you can genuinely rely on, get in touch and we will give you a fair, honest figure based on a proper look at your move.

faqs: Why do I need a survey for an accurate quote?

A survey lets us see the true volume of your belongings and the real access at both ends, including lofts, sheds and awkward staircases that a phone call cannot capture. Without it, a quote is essentially a guess that tends to turn into extra charges on the day.
Can I get a quote without anyone visiting?

Yes. A video walkthrough on your phone lets us see everything we need without arranging a visit, which suits busy households and longer-distance moves well. The key is that we see your home with our own eyes, however that is done, so the figure is accurate.
What information should I give for an accurate quote?

Show everything, including the loft, garage and shed, flag any large or awkward items, and be honest about access at both ends, especially the new home. Decide what packing and extras you want, and the quote will reflect your real move rather than an assumption.
Should I always get a quote in writing?

Always. A written quote removes any room for misunderstanding about what was agreed. Ask what is and is not included, whether protection and storage are covered, and use the answers to compare quotes like for like rather than by headline price alone.
